text: Apart from a couple of slips, the whole of this chapter can be unreservedly acclaimed for sustained quality. The first slip is the statement that Marlowe had to earn a not-for-export academic title, comparable to the Latin 'Ds.' or 'Dominus', by graduating BA, in order to be able to style himself 'Sir Christopher Marlowe' (11). In the biography on Marlowe by Constance Kuriyama, which this chapter follows closely along the lines of her 2002 biography, the academic title is explained as a similar English equivalent but Martin, rather than explaining the title, simply repeats the title with the honorific prefix, as if it was Marlowe's right to call himself a knight or baronet such as is exemplified in the relationships of the characters of Sir Toby Belch and Sir Andrew Aguecheek with that of the character of the faux-cleric, Sir Topas, in Shakespeare's Twelfth Night. The second slip is the characterization of 'conspiracy theories' the views of those scholars who, despite their differing opinion on Marlowe's character and the circumstances of his life, agree that Marlowe was murdered by agents of the state, rather than dying of natural causes after being killed in the course of an altercation over food, such as might have occurred over a lunch time argument, as conjectured by such scholars as David Riggs. The labelling of these alternative scholarly interpretations as 'conspiracy theories' seems to be an inappropriate way of presenting them.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
